question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageBatch()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the ByteBuffer.wrap(key) usage in findStorageBatch() — where the ByteBuffer shares the backing array with the original key byte[] — to cause a mutation of the key array after wrapping but before the batch query executes, resulting in the wrong slot being queried from contract_state and a corrupted historical state value being cached and served?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageBatch()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that findStorageBatch() iterates over cachedSlotKeys (which includes all previously requested slots for a contract) to cause a batch query that returns values for slots the current caller never requested, populating the contractStateCache with values for those slots and potentially overwriting previously cached historical values with current-state values?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageBatch()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the concurrent access to contractSlotsCache.getNativeCache().asMap().keySet() in findStorageBatch() — which is not synchronized — to cause a ConcurrentModificationException or read an inconsistent set of cached slot keys, resulting in a batch query that omits some slots and causes those slots to be served with incorrect (zero) historical values?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of validation that the provided blockTimestamp corresponds to an actual Hashgraph block (i.e., a real consensus_timestamp in the record_file table) to query contract state at a synthetic timestamp that falls between two real blocks, causing the mirror node to serve a state that was never the canonical Hashgraph state at any real block boundary?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the order by consensus_timestamp desc limit 1 in the findStorageByBlockTimestamp() SQL query to cause the query to return the most recent write before the given timestamp, even if that write was part of a failed or reverted transaction recorded in contract_state_change, thereby presenting a historically inaccurate state that includes reverted Hashgraph transactions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a blockTimestamp that is exactly 1 nanosecond before a known state-changing transaction's consensus_timestamp to cause findStorageByBlockTimestamp() to return the pre-transaction value, then use this to prove the existence of a specific transaction at a specific timestamp, enabling targeted Hashgraph history reconstruction attacks?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that findStorageByBlockTimestamp() does not validate that the provided EntityId corresponds to a contract (as opposed to an account or token) to query contract_state_change records for non-contract entity IDs, potentially retrieving state change records that were not intended to be exposed as contract storage history?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the long type for blockTimestamp (which can represent nanosecond-precision Hashgraph consensus timestamps) to supply a timestamp that overflows when converted to other time representations in downstream processing, causing the EVM simulation to use an incorrect historical state that does not correspond to any real Hashgraph block?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that contract_state_change records include both value_written and value_read columns, and that findStorageByBlockTimestamp() uses coalesce(value_written, value_read), to identify slots where value_written is NULL (read-only accesses) and use those timestamps to reconstruct which slots were read (but not written) during specific Hashgraph transactions, enabling storage layout inference attacks?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that findStorageByBlockTimestamp() returns value_read when value_written is NULL to cause an EVM simulation to use a stale pre-transaction value for a slot that was only read (not written) during a transaction, causing the simulation to produce results inconsistent with the actual Hashgraph-recorded post-transaction state?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of rate limiting on findStorageByBlockTimestamp() calls to perform a binary search over the blockTimestamp space to precisely identify the consensus_timestamp of specific Hashgraph transactions by observing when the returned storage value changes, enabling targeted timestamp-based attacks against Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that findStorageByBlockTimestamp() queries the contract_state_change table (which is partitioned by consensus_timestamp) with a very old blockTimestamp to cause the query to scan across multiple partitions, leading to excessive I/O that delays the processing of new Hashgraph blocks and creates a window of inconsistency in historical state queries?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the trimLeadingZeros() transformation applied to slotKeyByteArray in ContractStorageReadableKVState.readFromDataSource() before passing it to findStorageByBlockTimestamp(), causing two different 32-byte slot keys that differ only in leading zeros to resolve to the same trimmed key, resulting in one slot's historical value being returned for a different slot's query and corrupting the EVM's view of Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the asymmetry between findStorage() (which uses the full 32-byte key against contract_state) and findStorageByBlockTimestamp() (which uses trimLeadingZeros() key against contract_state_change) to cause a slot that exists in contract_state to not be found in contract_state_change at the same timestamp, creating a historical state inconsistency that misrepresents the Hashgraph-recorded contract storage?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the leftPadBytes() transformation applied to the result of findStorageByBlockTimestamp() in ContractStorageReadableKVState to cause a value shorter than 32 bytes stored in contract_state_change to be left-padded with zeros, making it appear as a different value than what was actually recorded in Hashgraph history, thereby presenting a tampered historical state to the EVM?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Optional.empty() return from findStorageByBlockTimestamp() — which causes ContractStorageReadableKVState.readFromDataSource() to return null — to cause the EVM to treat a contract slot as uninitialized (zero) at a historical timestamp when the Hashgraph history actually contains a non-zero value for that slot, leading to incorrect simulation results that misrepresent Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that findStorageByBlockTimestamp() does not check whether the contract was deployed before the given blockTimestamp to query storage state for a contract at a timestamp before its creation in Hashgraph history, causing the EVM to simulate the contract as having zero storage at a pre-deployment timestamp when no state should exist?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that findStorageByBlockTimestamp() does not check whether the contract was deleted before the given blockTimestamp to query storage state for a deleted contract at a post-deletion timestamp, causing the EVM to simulate the contract as having its last-known storage state even after it was deleted from Hashgraph, misrepresenting the post-deletion Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that findStorageByBlockTimestamp() passes entityId.getId() (a long) directly to the SQL query without validating that the ID corresponds to a contract entity type, to query contract_state_change records for an account ID, potentially retrieving state change records that were recorded under a different entity type and misrepresenting Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the nanosecond precision of blockTimestamp in findStorageByBlockTimestamp() to supply a timestamp that is within the same second as a real Hashgraph block but at a different nanosecond offset, causing the query to return a different historical state than what would be returned for the canonical block timestamp, enabling manipulation of which historical state is presented to the EVM?
